entities.cite: the sphota, ultimately the sabda-tattva of 1.1, is undivided

denials.cite: Neither ever presents its true face in ordinary transaction

claims.name: anukaroti
claims.cite: The imitation of differentiation by cognition

claims.name: kramopasrsta
claims.cite: speech has a form contaminated by sequence

rejected_readings.label: uncontaminated_speech
rejected_readings.rendering: Speech can appear in its pure undivided form, free from the contamination of sequence.
rejected_readings.why: Directly contradicts the verse's assertion that vak suffers upaplava constantly (dhruvam) and never appears except when contaminated by krama-upasrsta. The commentary explicitly denies that speech can present its true undivided face.

rejected_readings.label: undistorted_cognition
rejected_readings.rendering: In ordinary transaction, cognition presents its true undivided nature without imitating differentiation.
rejected_readings.why: Directly violates the verse's core doctrine that jnana perpetually imitates (anukaroti) bheda. The commentary states that neither cognition nor speech 'ever presents its true face in ordinary transaction', ruling out the possibility of unmediated presentation.

rejected_readings.label: cognition_independent
rejected_readings.rendering: Cognition is self-sufficient and does not depend upon objects for its operation.
rejected_readings.why: Violates the explicit claim that jnana is jneya-vyapasraya — cognition rests upon its objects. The mimicry of differentiation occurs precisely because cognition must conform to the structure of what it grasps.

notes: The verse marks the permanent condition of ordinary mind and speech: neither can present itself undistorted. Jnana (cognition) operates as a power that inevitably mimics differentiation; vak (speech) cannot escape sequence-contamination. This is not a limitation to be overcome but the structural basis for all ordinary knowledge—the distortion is the means (upaya). The sort assignment strains slightly: jnana as 'power' bridges its ultimate undividedness with its functional necessity of mimicry; vak as 'manifestation' acknowledges that speech's true nature (sphota/sabdatattva) is absolute yet only appears in divided form. This tension mirrors the philosophical paradox the verse articulates.
